coredata.dc:title: Tuning the luminescence of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots synthesized by pulsed laser ablation in liquid and their use as a selective photoluminescence on–off–on probe for ascorbic acid detection
coredata.dc:creator: Novoa-De León, I.C.
Johny, J.
Vázquez-Rodríguez, S.
García-Gómez, N.
Carranza-Bernal, S.
Mendivil, I.
Shaji, S.
Sepúlveda-Guzmán, S.
coredata.dc:description: In this work, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were synthesized by pulsed laser ablation in liquid using Nd:YAG laser (532 nm). Graphite target was ablated in dimethylformamide, as solvent and nitrogen source, and the microstructure as well as optical properties of N-GQDs were studied. The N-GQDs structure consists of a graphitic core with oxygen and nitrogen functionalities and particle size about 3 nm, as demonstrated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy and transmission electron microscopy. The as-prepared N-GQDs structure was modified by solvothermal treatment at 65, 90 and 120 °C reducing the oxygen functional groups, adding nitrogen and restoring the π-conjugated structure of N-GQD. The N-GQDs exhibit UV–Vis absorption spectrum with the characteristic π-π* and n – π* electronic transitions of the GQDs with a large amount of oxygen and nitrogen functionalities. These N-GQDs exhibited a visible light photoluminescence centered at 486 nm upon an excitation of 410 nm and the photoluminescence intensity enhanced up to 4.05% of quantum yield after solvothermal treatment. The N-GQDs dispersion was used for selective detection of ascorbic acid, through a signal-off and signal-on system. The results show the use of N-GQDs as a competent photoluminescence sensor for metal ions and ascorbic acid.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence quenching
Carbon nanoparticles
Electron transfer

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) XPS C 1s of N-GQDs before and after solvothermal treatment, b) XPS N 1s of N-GQDs before and after solvothermal treatment, c) Scheme of incorporation of nitrogen into the N-GQDs structure after solvothermal treatment. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a) Raman spectra of N-GQDs before and after solvothermal treatment, b) FTIR spectra in the range of 2000–1000 cm−1 of N-GQDs before and after solvothermal treatment, c) FTIR spectra in the range of 4000–2000 cm−1 of N-GQDs before and after solvothermal treatment. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a) TEM image of the as-prepared N-GQDs. The inset is the respective particle size distribution. b) HRTEM image of as-prepared N-GQDs. c) SAED pattern of as-prepared N-GQDs. d) TEM image of the T120 N-GQDs. The inset is the respective particle size distribution. e) HRTEM image of T120 N-GQDs. f) SAED pattern of T120 N-GQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of N-GQDs, b) Fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs before and after solvothermal treatment. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Deconvolution of fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs before and after solvothermal treatment showing the contributions of π → π* and n → π* radiative transitions. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a) Fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs in the presence of different concentration of Fe3+. Inset shows linear relationship between fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs and the Fe3+ concentration; b) Comparation of fluorescence quenching of N-GQDs induced by Fe3+ and other metal ions at 1000 μM concentration. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a) Fluorescence spectra of N-QDs/Fe3+ in the presence of different concentration of AA. Inset shows linear relationship between fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs/Fe3+ and the concentration of AA, (b) Selectivity of AA detection based on fluorescence of N-GQDs/Fe3+ fluorescence intensities of the N-GQDs/Fe3+ in the absence and presence of AA and other substances. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The detection limits of different methods for the determination of AA.

head.ce:title: Tuning the luminescence of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots synthesized by pulsed laser ablation in liquid and their use as a selective photoluminescence on–off–on probe for ascorbic acid detection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centro de Investigación en Materiales Avanzados
Parque de Investigación e Innovación Tecnológica

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centro de Investigacion en Materiales Avanzados. Alianza Norte 202. Parque de Investigacion e Innovacion Tecnologica. Apodaca, 66600, Nuevo Leon, Mexico

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were synthesized by pulsed laser ablation in liquid using Nd:YAG laser (532 nm). Graphite target was ablated in dimethylformamide, as solvent and nitrogen source, and the microstructure as well as optical properties of N-GQDs were studied. The N-GQDs structure consists of a graphitic core with oxygen and nitrogen functionalities and particle size about 3 nm, as demonstrated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy and transmission electron microscopy. The as-prepared N-GQDs structure was modified by solvothermal treatment at 65, 90 and 120 °C reducing the oxygen functional groups, adding nitrogen and restoring the π-conjugated structure of N-GQD. The N-GQDs exhibit UV–Vis absorption spectrum with the characteristic π-π* and n – π* electronic transitions of the GQDs with a large amount of oxygen and nitrogen functionalities. These N-GQDs exhibited a visible light photoluminescence centered at 486 nm upon an excitation of 410 nm and the photoluminescence intensity enhanced up to 4.05% of quantum yield after solvothermal treatment. The N-GQDs dispersion was used for selective detection of ascorbic acid, through a signal-off and signal-on system. The results show the use of N-GQDs as a competent photoluminescence sensor for metal ions and ascorbic acid.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), is a new member of the graphene family discovered very recently as a zero-dimensional class of graphitic nanomaterials. GQDs are small fragments of carbonaceous materials with diameter below 10 nm and graphitic or turbostratic sp2-carbon structure core, exhibiting quantum size effect and exciton confinement [1,2]. GQDs display interesting fluorescence properties, variable size dependent band gaps and biocompatibility; in addition, the GQDs also contain oxygen functionalities that allow their water solubility [2,3]. In recent years, the GQDs have been studied deeply finding that the photoluminescence (PL) emission may be derived from electron-hole recombination, quantum size effect, doping, and surface defects in the functional groups of GQDs [1,3]. Consequently, the PL emission is strongly dependent on microstructure and morphology induced mainly during the nanoparticles synthesis and in that sense, several approaches have been explored to synthesize GQDs with control in morphology and microstructure such as solvothermal, hydrothermal, electrochemical synthesis, ultrasonic and pyrolysis of organic compounds [4–9]. However, most of these methods involve complex and time-consuming pretreatment and purification processes. Lately, a lot of interest has been paid in tuning the optical properties, surface and chemical features of GQDs by doping with heteroatoms like N, B, Si, and S [10–13]. In graphene-like structures, the electron density changes due to doping with electron donor (n-type) or electron-acceptor atoms (p-type) which eventually influence their optical and electrical properties [14,15]. The n-type doping favors the radiative relaxation pathways of GQDs increasing the quantum yield unlike p-type doping [16]. Nitrogen atom is often introduced in GQDs structure due to its comparable atomic size to carbon atom improving the optical properties particularly exhibiting broad band emission with high fluorescence quantum yield. N-GQDs can be successfully prepared by several approaches like those used for the synthesis of pure GQDs [10,17,18]. However, in most of the cases, diversified complex methods with long reaction time or elevated temperatures and more expensive precursors are needed. Pulsed laser ablation in liquid (PLAL) was first reported by Patil and co-worker in 1987 and it is a widely used technique to synthesize various nanoparticles through a single step [19,20]. In this method, a solid target is immersed in a liquid medium and the laser beam is focused through the liquid onto the target surface. During operation, the interaction between short (∼10−13 to 10−8 s) and intense (∼106–1014 W/cm2) laser pulses and the interface between target surface and liquid generate capsules with elevated temperature and pressures resulting in material vaporization and ejection of surface material that allows nucleation and nanoparticles growth [21,22]. This method is simple, fast, and it is considered as a green method to produce highly pure nanocolloids usually with the same composition as the target. A few works about pulsed laser ablation of solid carbon materials in liquid have been reported for the synthesis of carbon dots and GQDs through one-step and environmental-friendly method [23–25]. In addition, carbon nanoparticles synthesized by PLAL exhibit optical properties with solvent dependence since the surface can be modified or functionalized by the interaction with the solvent [18,26]. Pegylated GQDs were obtained in-situ by pulsed laser ablation of a graphite target in polyethylene glycol water solution for their direct application as in vivo fluorescent image agent [27]. Nitrogen doping of GQDs has also been reported by PLAL using a water dispersion of graphene oxide as carbon source and diethylenetriamine as N source [18]. This has opened the opportunity to generate surface passivation and doping with heteroatoms that in turn can improve the quantum yield of GQDs.

Ascorbic acid (AA) is a strong antioxidant that can reduce oxidative stress in the body and therefore believed to be able to lower cancer and cardiovascular risks. In addition, AA is essentially taken in our daily diet to fulfill the body requirement for the development and maintenance of scar tissue, blood vessels, for the formation of collagen and cartilage; also plays an important role in the absorption of iron and the lack of it increases the susceptibility to many kinds of infections [28,29]. Besides, AA is involved directly in creating a series of other small biomolecules such as adenosine triphosphate, dopamine, peptide hormones, etc. [28]. Therefore, analytical methods for AA detection are of great significance. The fluorescence assays display unique advantages of high sensitivity, great simplicity, easy monitoring, and rapid response, providing a better choice for the detection of AA.

Compared to organic fluorescent probes and semi-conductive quantum dots, GQDs are superior in terms of their remarkable optical properties, such as high photostability against photobleaching and blinking, biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and low cost properties, which makes them suitable for bio-imaging, fluorescent sensing, and photovoltaic devices [17,30–34]. The fluorescent sensors using GQDs are based on the change in fluorescence intensity by the analyte. Various sensors based on GQDs have been fabricated recently with either turn off or turn on processes [35–37] The turn off sensing originates from the energy and/or electron transfer from GQDs to the analytes quenching the GQDs fluorescence, but this process can be influenced by other parameters leading in false positives unlike turn on sensing, that recently has received more attention [32,38,39]. Recent studies indicate that n-type doping of GQD accelerates the electron transfer (ET) process due to extra free electrons incorporation in the structure [16].

In this study, we developed an easy, efficient, and green route to prepare N-doped GQDs by PLAL using graphite target as carbon source and dimethylformamide as liquid medium of ablation and nitrogen source, followed by solvothermal treatment. Morphology and structural characterization by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) revealed the graphitic structure and small particle size. A detailed structural study was carried out on the N-GQDs before and after solvothermal treatment by Raman microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy to explore their surface oxidation and PL intensity. Fluorescent sensors with turn off and turn on features based on N-GQDs were also developed. The fluorescence of N-GQDs was quenched by Fe3+ through electron transfer mechanism. The turn on fluorescence sensor was developed for AA because AA induces the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+, which leads to the blocking of ET. This fluorescent sensor made with N-GQDs provides a better choice for the detection of AA.

A graphite target was prepared by cryomilling of graphite powder using a freezer mill 6770 Spex SamplePrep, and then 1 g of cryomilled graphite was placed into a 1.2 cm die and hard-pressed at 24000 psi. The resulting pellet was annealed at 800 °C during 3 h under argon (Ar) flow. Raman analysis and morphology characterization of graphite target are shown in Fig. S1. N-GQDs were synthesized by irradiating the graphite target with a pulsed Nd:YAG laser (Solar laser systems LQ-629, 10 ns pulse width) of 532 nm wavelength and of 100 Hz (pulse repetition rate) in dimethylformamide (DMF) as liquid medium. The laser output was focused onto the target surface using a convex lens (focal length 10 cm), which was kept 12 cm away from the target and the energy fluence was 7.5 Jcm−2. The amount of solids in the dispersion was very low making it unable to be measured gravimetrically but the ablation experiments were carried out to produce dispersions with an absorbance intensity maximum of 3.2 at 278 nm. After PLAL, a brown colloidal dispersion was obtained and then subjected to solvothermal treatment using a sealed glass bottle (250 mL) in to an oven at 65, 90 and 120 °C during 72 h.

The optical properties of the N-GQDs were studied using a UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometer Cary 6000i. The photoluminescence characterization was carried out using a Luminescence spectrometer PerkinElmer LS55, acquiring emission spectra in the range 440–600 nm upon excitation for each sample. Raman spectra were obtained using a Thermo Scientific DXR Raman microscope. The sample dispersion was dried on a silicon wafer and observed with a 100× objective, the Raman spectrum was acquired using an excitation laser line of 740 nm. Morphology and microstructural characterization were done using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) FEI Titan G2 80–300. Drops of sample dispersion were dried on a 200 mesh Cu grid coated with an ultrathin carbon film. The grid was placed in the TEM and observed at 300 KV of accelerating voltage. The ζ-potential of samples was obtained using a Particle Size and Z Potential Analyzer Zetasizer Nano ZS. The elemental composition and chemical states of the samples were identified by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha- X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer System. The analysis was done with monochromatized Al Kα radiation (E = 1486.68 eV). The sample dispersion was dried on a Si wafer and fixed with Cu tape on the XPS sample holder. The analysis was carried out acquiring the survey spectra with a pass energy of 200 eV and high-resolution spectra for C1s, N1s, and O1s using a pass energy of 50 eV. The charge correction was done using the C1s binding energy at 284.6 eV [40]. A non-linear fitting was performed using Gaussian function; the full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) was kept constant for all components in a high-resolution spectrum. The sample dispersions were analyzed using a Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer Shimadzu IRTracer-100 equipped with attenuated total reflectance (ATR) accessory. Aliquots of the dispersion were dropped on a diamond crystal and the spectra were acquired in a wavenumber range of 4000–600 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1.

The quantum yield (QY) of N-GQDs was calculated following equation (1):
Q Y = Q
Y
R
×
I
x
I
R
×
A
R
A
X
×
n
x
2
n
R
2
where, I is the integrated fluorescence emission intensity, n is the refractive index, and A is the optical absorbance. The subscripts X and R refer to the sample and the reference fluorophore of known quantum yield respectively. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (Q = 0.54 at 340 nm) was chosen as a reference fluorophore [41].

For the detection of Fe3+, FeNO3·H2O has been used. The solution containing a calculated amount of ions was added into N-GQDs dispersion. The PL spectra were recorded after reaction for 1 min and then the fluorescence intensity was measured at λex = 410 nm using a luminescence spectrometer PerkinElmer LS55 with a quartz cell of 3 mL.

For the determination of sensing properties for AA, the following procedure was carried out. A 2.7 ml volume of N-GQDs with 1000 μM Fe3+ was dispersed with different concentrations of AA. The mixture was incubated for 1 min at room temperature, and then the fluorescence intensity was measured at λex = 410 nm.

A brown colloidal dispersion was obtained after laser ablation of graphite target in DMF. The colloids exhibit high colloidal stability for more than 10 months as no precipitation or agglomeration was noticed. The ζ-potential (ζ) values for the as-prepared carbon colloids and after solvothermal treatment samples (T65, T90 and T120) exhibited negative values (Table S1). This negative value of ζ-potential may be due to an electron cloud around nanoparticles arising from possible oxygen functional groups and N doping atoms in the GQDs structure which enhance hydrophilicity and stability in the DMF medium [42–44]. The N doping of carbon colloids might result from using DMF as N source during PLAL and the subsequent solvothermal treatment in the same solvent. The chemical composition and functionalities of N-GQDs were analyzed by XPS before and after solvothermal treatment. Survey spectrum for the as-prepared GQD sample shows that the elemental composition consists mainly of C and O, however, after solvothermal treatment, the N 1s photoelectron peak appears and its intensity increases as the treatment temperature increases (Fig. S2). The analysis of high-resolution spectra of C 1s and N 1s are shown in Fig. 1 . The non-linear fitting for C1s spectrum resulted in four peaks for the as-prepared sample associated with CC, C–C (284.5 eV), C–N (285.6 eV), C–O (286.2 eV), and CO (287.3 eV) species [7,10,45,46]. The results suggest that besides oxygen functional groups there are some nitrogen species in the as-prepared sample which confirms that nitrogen and oxygen atoms were effectively included into the structure of GQDs during the synthesis by PLA of graphite target in DMF as source of N and O. The C 1s spectra for the T65, T90 and T120 samples show a decrease in the intensity of C–O (286.2 eV), and CO (287.3 eV) species due to the removal of oxygen functional groups by the solvothermal treatment. The fraction of oxygenated species (C–O, CO) from the C 1s spectra decreased from 0.3 for the as-prepared sample to 0.18 for the T120 sample, while the fraction of C–C and CC species increased from 0.55 to 0.65 with the solvothermal treatment (Table S1). In addition, the fraction of C–N species also increases to 0.17, which suggests that nitrogen doping occurs during oxygen removal by the solvothermal treatment in DMF at 120 °C. In addition, N atoms can dope graphene in three configurations during PLAL: graphitic, pyrrolic and amino [47], and to elucidate C–N bonding in the GQDs the N 1s spectra for the samples synthesized by PLAL before and after solvothermal treatment were analyzed (Fig. 1b). The N 1s spectrum of the as-prepared sample, in spite of the low intensity, shows two peaks, the amino-like and graphitic-like [48], incorporated to the N-GQDs structure during the ablation process. The nanoparticles synthesized by PLAL usually present a similar chemical composition as the target, however; this result suggests that a reactive system was created in presence of DMF changing the composition of resulting nanoparticles. In addition, during the solvothermal treatment in DMF also nitrogen gets doped into the GQDs structure as shown by the increasing fraction of C–N species as indicated in other reports [18,46,49]. This was corroborated by the analysis of N 1s spectra for the T65, T90 and T120 samples. The solvothermal treatment embed N species as pyrrolic-like structure and the respective peak (400.1 eV) exhibits the highest contribution in the T120 sample. The results are further supported by the surface elemental composition from the XPS analysis, the nitrogen atomic % increased from 8 for the as-prepared sample to 18 for the sample with thermal treatment at 120 °C (Table S1). The oxygen fraction decreased as a function of the thermal treatment temperature supporting the deoxygenation. The results show that the chemical composition is first influenced by the laser ablation parameters and liquid media; however, thermal treatment also played a significant role in determining the final composition since higher temperature favored better nitrogen incorporation and oxygen removal in comparison with others. Fig. 1c shows the N-GQDs chemical structure based on the XPS results before and after solvothermal treatment. PLAL of a graphite target results in atoms and ions expelled out of the target and the particle formation proceeds by the combination of several processes like the collision of ablated atoms, radicals, ions and clusters [21] in the plasma confined by the solvent (DMF). A thin layer of the solvent closer to the plasma gets evaporated and the interaction of laser induced plasma with the species in DMF can occur which may lead to the generation of GQD samples having high amount of oxygen functional groups together with some nitrogen species as graphitic-like and amino-like structures. The results agree with previous studies on the role of liquid on the composition of nanoparticles by PLAL where oxygen is incorporated in the nanoparticles by using an oxygen-rich solvent [50]. After solvothermal treatment, nitrogen species increased with the pyrrolic-like structure through the reduction of oxygen functional groups of the N-GQDs in DMF.

Further structural characterization was carried out by Raman microscopy and FTIR spectroscopy. Fig. 2 a shows the Raman spectrum for the as-prepared N-GQDs sample exhibiting the G band (1565 cm−1) related to the in plane stretching vibration of sp2 carbon atoms and the D band (1311 cm−1), which is recognized as the disordered band due to structural defects, edge and dangling sp2 carbon bonds that break the symmetry [51]. The intensity of the G band is related to the amount of sp2 domains whereas the D band to the sp3 domains, which suggests that the oxygen and nitrogen functional groups contributes to increase the disordered structure in N-GQDs for the as-prepared sample [52,53]. The Raman spectra of the as-prepared sample agree with that of graphite target (Fig. S1a), however, the intensity ratios of D and G bands (ID/IG) revealed that the N-GQDs exhibit a larger contribution of defects (0.55) than those present in the graphite target (0.23) possibly due to the edges and the oxygen functionalities. Besides, the D band intensity decreases as the treatment temperature increases, and it vanishes in the spectrum of the T120 sample. The ID/IG ratio decreased from 0.55 for the as-prepared N-GQDs to 0.36, 0.24 and 0.062 for T60, T90 and T120 samples respectively. The ID/IG ratio decreases due to the removal of oxygen functional groups as observed by the XPS analysis but also due to the increased sp2 domains. These results show that not only removal of oxygen functional groups occurs during solvothermal treatment but also the recovering of π structure in the GQDs [53]. In addition, the spectra also show a small shoulder at 1625 cm−1 associated with the D’ band commonly observed in N-doped graphene materials synthesized by bottom up techniques [54].

The samples were also analyzed using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and the spectra are shown in Fig. 2b. The FTIR spectrum of the as-prepared sample exhibits the CO stretching band at 1714 cm−1, CC stretching band at 1658 cm−1 and two vibration bands at 1385 and 1255 cm−1 due to the stretching of C–N bond and the stretching double peak of C–O at 1088 and 1064 cm−1, it also shows the O–H at 3506 cm−1 (Fig. 2c). After solvothermal treatment, the N-GQDs spectra exhibit an increase of C–N stretching band intensity associated with the incorporation of nitrogen into the structure, in addition, new doublet bands at 2929 and 2851 cm−1 related to N–H bending in the amine group are observed. The spectra for N-GQDs after solvothermal treatment exhibit a decrease in the intensity of the CO and O–H stretching bands due to deoxygenation (Fig. 2c) and mainly the T120 sample spectrum, the bands are illegible. However, some oxygen functional groups remain in the N-GQDs evidenced by the C–O stretching band in the spectra. These results agree with those obtained by XPS and confirm the chemical composition, the removal of oxygen functional groups and the nitrogen doping in the N-GQDs structure by the solvothermal treatment in DMF. The results also support the negative values of ζ-potential of N-GQDs, which arise from the abundance of functional groups (Table S1).

The morphology and microstructure of N-GQDs were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 3 a shows the low-magnification image of the as-prepared N-GQDs exhibiting agglomeration due to the drying process. However, the high-resolution TEM image exhibits isolated nanocrystals with small particle size ranging from 1.5 to 7.5 nm, with an average size of 4.5 nm (Fig. 3a inset). The image also displays the lattice fringes with spacing of 0.20, 0.21, and 0.245 nm associated with (100) plane of diamond-like structure and (101) plane of graphitic-like structure (Fig. 3c). This suggests that the laser ablation of a graphite target in DMF results in N-GQDs with more than one crystalline structure. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) of the as-prepared N-GQDs exhibited diffraction rings due to the presence of nanocrystals randomly oriented. The diffraction rings were indexed and most of them are associated with the graphitic structure, but some diffraction spots were also related to the diamond-like structure according with the JCPDS No. 751621 and 791467 respectively. The TEM results show that laser ablation of a graphite target in DMF resulted in N-GQD with crystalline graphitic-like structure and a small fraction with diamond-like structure and some amorphous carbon; however, the dominant features correspond to the graphitic structure. The non-equilibrium conditions for nucleation and growth of carbon-based nanostructures by laser ablation/irradiation are favorable for the synthesis of carbon allotropes that are not commonly synthesized under the typical methods [55]. In this case, besides the synthesis of graphitic-like nanoparticles, the process might be favorable for the evaporation of carbon atoms and their restructuring into other allotropes. Particularly, according with previous reports the growth of diamond crystalline structure occurs during the early stage of plasma in the time lapse of peak pressure induced by the shockwave [55]. Fig. 3d–f shows the TEM analysis of the T120 sample. The low-magnification image shows particles agglomerated whereas the HRTEM image reveals that the particle size remains unchanged therefore the solvothermal treatment does not induce any particle aggregation. (inset Fig. 3d). The lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.20 and 0.21 nm revealed the presence of (100) plane and (101) plane in the graphitic-like structure. In addition, after thermal treatment, SAED of T120 sample exhibits larger amount of diffraction spots corresponding to the crystalline planes of graphitic-like structure such as (110), (104) and (002) planes. This is due to particles with metastable structure and low dimensionality is more likely to change to graphitic-like structure with the thermal treatment. (Fig. 3f). The morphology and structure analysis of T65 and T90 samples are shown in the supplementary material (Fig. S6). These results agree with previous experiments by PLAL using water as liquid that achieved the production of carbon nanoparticles with particle size ranging between 15 and 20 nm, and mixed crystalline structure including diamond-like structure (5–40%) and graphite-like structure [55,56].

The findings during structural studies of N-GQDs by Raman and TEM analysis demonstrated the presence of a graphitic core unlike of an amorphous core in carbon dots. In addition, XPS, and FTIR spectroscopy show the composition and tuning of surface of N-GQDs in oxygen and nitrogen functionalities. Raman analysis also demonstrated an increase in the π-conjugated structure after the solvothermal treatment in DMF.

The effect of tuning N-GQDs surface on its optical properties was studied by UV–Vis–NIR and Photoluminescence spectroscopy. Fig. 4 a presents the optical absorption spectrum of the as-prepared sample which has a main absorption at 278 nm, ascribed to the π → π* transition assigned to the aromatic sp2 domains in the N-GQDs structure [43,46,57,58] and four absorption bands at 328, 355, 377 and 428 nm are attributed to the n → π* transitions due to the presence of oxygenated groups like C–O, epoxy and CO at the edge of the N-GQDs [7,49,59]. The spectroscopic results by XPS, Raman and FTIR showed that the solvothermal treatment of N-GQDs in DMF induced the chemical reduction of oxygen functional groups restoring the conjugated structure. This is also supported by the optical absorption spectra of the samples after solvothermal treatment at 65, 90 and 120 °C. The spectra show a progressive change in the optical absorption due to the change in chemical functionalities with the temperature treatment. All samples exhibit absorption bands associated to the π → π* transition, however, the T65 sample spectrum, unlike the as-prepared nanocolloid spectrum, shows three bands associated with the n → π* transitions. For the T90 sample spectrum, only one n → π* transition band is observed at 319 nm, but when the treatment temperature reaches 120 °C, T120 sample spectrum exhibits the absorption band associated with the π → π* transition at 278 nm only. These results suggest the tuning of surface oxidation and structure modification of N-GQDs with the solvothermal treatment results in a highly conjugated structure that agree with the Raman results where the G band is dominant [46,49].

The photoluminescence (PL) analysis of as-prepared N-GQDs dispersion was carried out under wavelength of excitation from 320 to 410 nm (Fig. S3 ) and a maximum visible light emission at 486 nm was obtained under excitation wavelength of 410 nm. This emission is related to relaxation of excited states due to π → π* transition and n → π* transitions [1,60]. The sample however, presents a low PL intensity which increases after solvothermal treatment as a function of the temperature due to the reduction of oxygen functional groups (Fig. 4b). This was further supported by the calculation of PL quantum yield (QY), which increases from 0.60% for the as-prepared sample to 0.91, 1.74 and 4.05% for the T65, T90 and T120 samples respectively. The enhancement of PL QY of the N-GQDs occurred due to the removal of the oxygen functional groups from the N-GQDs edges and the enriched electron density of the π structure by restoring of conjugated domains that suppress the non-radiative recombination [1]. These results agree with the previous assignments and show the tuning of oxygenated surface of N-GQDs strongly modify the optical absorption and PL intensity [43,46,49]. In addition, the values obtained for QY are comparable to other reports [58,61] available. Additional PL characterization by acquiring the excitation spectra of the samples (Fig. S4) revealed that the as-prepared sample presents two excitation bands at 368 nm and 406 nm, attributed to the disordered structure consisting of large amount of oxygen functional groups and defects, and the π structure. After solvothermal treatment, the excitation spectra show only one excitation peak, due to the removal of oxygen functional groups. The PL characterization of the rest of the samples show that while the highest PL intensity was found for excitation wavelength of 406 nm, the optimal excitation for the T120 sample and its emission wavelength shifted to 370 nm and 449 nm respectively (Fig. S5). This could be associated with the large domains of π structure in that sample, as shown in the UV–Vis spectrum, shifting the maximum excitation wavelength to UV region.

A non-linear fitting was performed to the PL emission spectra (λex = 410 nm) using a Lorenzian function to study the radiative combinations in the N-GQDs dispersions (Fig. 5 ). The N-GQDs own a graphene core and surface with abundant functional groups. The PL is controlled by both; the graphene core and the surrounding chemical functionalities. According to previous reports, the PL emission spectra for graphene materials can be deconvoluted in two peaks; peak I, ascribed to the n → π* transition originated from the oxygen and nitrogen functional groups such as carbonyl, amine, and ethers [62], and peak II assigned to the π → π* transition in the sp2 domains due to CC bonds in the graphene π-conjugated network [57,63]. The results show that the as-prepared N-GQDs spectrum exhibits a similar contribution of both, n → π* and π → π* transitions, due to the large amount of oxygen functional groups. However, the intensity of peak I decreases with a significant increase in the intensity of peak II, as the solvothermal temperature increases, due to the restoring of sp2 domains associated with the π → π* transition. Peak II is remarkably dominant in T120 sample spectrum and there is still a contribution of peak I indicating the presence of remaining functional groups and defects that cause n → π* radiative combinations after the solvothermal treatment. This also supports the N-doping in the graphene structure of N-GQDs, due to the presence of DMF during nanoparticles synthesis and subsequent addition of amine groups during the reduction of oxygen functional groups by solvothermal treatment that finally contributes to PL peak II intensity.

The functional groups of N-GQDs can be influenced by metal ions tuning the PL intensity of N-GQDs. This feature makes N-GQDs suitable as a PL sensor for metal ions. Based on the characterization results, the T90 sample was selected for the detection experiments because of the remaining oxygen functional groups that contribute to water solubility and might interact with metal ions and molecules through electrostatic interaction; also T90 sample has a QY enough to be detected by the fluorometer [64]. N-GQDs show strong PL intensity upon excitation of 410 nm. In order to prepare a turn on detector, the PL intensity of N-GQDs system should be quenched. Fe3+ is an essential trace element that plays a significant role in chemical and biological processes, Fe3+ fluctuations are the hallmarks of diseases such as anemia, intelligence decline, arthritis, heart failure, diabetes and cancer [65,66]. Current detection assays including voltammetry [67], spectrophotometry [68], atomic absorption spectrometry [68], require sophisticated instrumentation and tedious sample preparation procedures. In the presence of Fe3+, the PL of N-GQDs was quenched significantly through the electron transfer process that involves partial transfer of an electron in the excited state to the d orbital of Fe3+ [5,64]. The Fe3+ ions bind to N-GQDs through the formation of complexes between Fe3+ ions and the phenolic hydroxyl groups [5]. Fig. 6 shows the PL spectra of N-GQDs dispersion during the detection experiments of Fe3+ as analyte. The molar concentration of the Fe3+ ranged from 1 to 1000 μM, while the fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs gradually decreased. Fig. 6a shows the dependence of PL on the concentration of Fe3+ ions in terms of F0–F; where F0 is the maximum PL intensity of N-GQDs dispersion and F is the PL intensity measured after each addition of Fe3+ solution. Interestingly, the PL intensity of the N-GQDs shows a good linear relationship between the PL intensity (F0–F) and the concentration of Fe3+ ([C]) over the range 1–100 μM, above 100 μM the relationship loses linearity (inset in Fig. 6a). The calculation of the limit of detection (LOD) was carried out from the linear fitting of the data resulting in F0–F = 5.94[C]+ 62.29, with a correlation coefficient of 0.991. Under this condition, the LOD was 5.52 nM based on 3 σ/k; σ the standard deviation of blank measurement with n = 5 correspond to the standard deviation obtained from five measurements and k is the slop between the fluorescence intensity versus concentration. The resulting LOD value is similar to the values reported in most of the previous works for Fe3+ detection [5,12,45,58]. The results indicate the excellent sensing properties of N-GQDs in the detection of trace Fe 3+. The PL intensity decreased to 88.2% using a Fe3+ concentration of 1000 μM, therefore, 1000 μM Fe3+ was selected as an optimal concentration for the quenched system of N-GQDs/Fe3+.

The selectivity of the fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ was studied using the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) of N-GQDs in presence of other metal ions such as Ag+, Na+, K+, Au3+, Co2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, As3+ and Ni2+ (Fig. 6b). These results agree with the previous reports where metal ions with smaller ionic radius show larger effect in the fluorescence intensity of carbonaceous materials [69]. The presence of each metal ion influenced the PL intensity; however, Fe3+ still shows the strongest quenching effect of the PL intensity of N-GQDs due to unlike other metal ions, Fe3+ ions bind to N-GQDs through the formation of complexes with the phenolic hydroxyl groups making the electron transfer mechanism more efficient. This result indicates that the N-GQDs show high selectivity and sensitivity for Fe3+ detection.

The selectivity of the sensor over other biomolecules as l-cysteine (CYS), uric acid (UA), glucose (GLU), and dopamine (DOP) was studied. Fig. 7b shows the poor effect of the biomolecules on PL of N-GQDs/Fe3+ system. This result suggests that this system is adaptable for the biological sample analysis. In addition, the selectivity of the AA/N-GQDs/Fe3+ system was confirmed testing the AA detection by other N-GQDs/metal ions systems. In that sense, the change in the PL was evaluated in terms of delta, that is, the mathematical difference between the maximum PL intensity of AA/N-GQDs/metal ion and the maximum PL emission of N-GQDs/metal ion system, finding that due to the large electron transfer effect between N-GQDs/Fe3+ the redox reaction with AA is more efficient for Fe3+ (Fig. S7). Finally, the pathway to prepare N-GQDs dispersion presented in this work has potential for large production of graphene-based nanomaterials with tuned optical properties as function of structure and morphology; and the production of competent biocompatible fluorescence sensors.

N-doped GQD with tuned surface oxidation and fluorescence emission, were prepared by an efficient two-step method. One-step through the PLAL of a graphite target in DMF medium that acts also as the nitrogen source. PLAL allows the high production of a stable colloidal dispersion of N-GQDs. The subsequent solvothermal treatment in DMF reduced the oxygen functional groups, increased the nitrogen content in N-GQDs and restored the π -conjugated structure as demonstrated by various characterization techniques. The optical properties of N-GQDs were increased dramatically, exhibiting excellent photoluminescence intensity reaching a QY of 4.05% after solvothermal treatment at 120 °C. These N-GQDs present selective and sensitive photoluminescence “on–off–on” performance to detect the presence of AA in aqueous solution. The fluorescence quenching of N-GQDs (turn off) occurred in the presence of Fe3+, but with the addition of AA the fluorescence was recovered (turn on). This method with high sensitivity, selectivity, low cost and visible optical detection may find broad applications in detection of AA in aqueous solutions. These results will be beneficial for further development of turn on nano-sensors based on graphene quantum dots/Fe3+ system for the detection of neurotransmitters or antibiotics, which present Fe3+ complexing properties.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge CONACYT for the financial support through CB-239354 project. I.C.N.D.L. thanks CONACYT-Mexico for the scholarship for Doctoral studies. The authors also thank M.C. Lila M. Bautista Carrillo for the technical assistance in ζ-potential measurements.
